speaker: interviewer
text: Huppert. Tape number six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Huppert, what are we seeing here? Who are we seeing?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's another picture of myself after the war, right after the war. I was in DP camp in Deggendorf. That was the first time they made pictures of us. And that's-- what was 1945? Oh no, '46. I assume that my husband has this on the DP card. That's my picture. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's our wedding, wedding-- the day where we got married in Deggendorf, my husband and I, Moris. And I regret that we didn't have a-- with the larger picture to show the gown. My husband went to all the trouble through to find it for me. That time was very difficult. And my husband gathered from Czechoslovakian princess, which traveled-- this wedding gown traveled all over Germany to every bride that got married, because they had no gowns. And-- but-- with-- in this picture I had wonderful people, which on the left-hand side is [? Supka ?], which a cousin of my husband's wife. And she was very dear to me, precious. And we loved each other very much and respect each other. She survived the time by a priest, as a-- as a-- a Gentile. But we live very close to each other. We love each other very much. And on the right-hand side, between the lights, is my mom's relative, cousin, which she, surviving, came to the wedding. It's too bad I don't have the other lady from Israel, Mrs. [? Megorska, ?] who she was very dear person, helping with the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother and my sister-in-law in Paris, which they survived in Siberia near [NON-ENGLISH]. When my daddy saved his life from-- from the concentration camp, and they met in Lemberg together. They registered and they got to Siberia. It was in the spies. And I got this picture after the war, 1955. And I cherished-- they went through very difficult time in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your sister-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen. She is a wonderful, intelligent, very bright, full of life person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this we're seeing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is when we went for the gathering from Sosnowiec. All the Sosnowiec people who have survived from the Holocaust together from all over the world. They came to this gathering. And my brother came from Paris. My cousin, Lola, which she wasn't from Sosnowiec, but she's really been close to us. She's the only survivor also from the family, Lola Gold from Sweicko, where my mom came. And it's my husband-- and standing right next to me is Moris, my husband. In the back, Josef Zarasinska, that's my brother, and that is next to me also. And on this side to my cousin is her husband, [? Heshig ?]. [? Heringold ?] now from her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Lola's husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry? Lola's husband, right. And in the back of us, the sign says 'The Horror'. That's a good name to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this gathering?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1989. When they-- also some of these people came to Israel to honor this-- to-- to-- to commemorate the date of our-- to-- of the Sosnowiec gathering, all Sosnowiec. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Huppert, what is this that you're showing us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the tie from my-- my daddy's tie, which he forgot he wore it when he took my brother to-- to-- the Russians. Tried to save my brother's life from camp, from concentration camp. He smuggled him through the border, tried to save him. And when before he-- my daddy came back, but he wore this tie, and he must have forgotten. He left the tie in Lemberg with my brother. And forgot also a girdle, what they called-- it's like a sash, which they used to for Friday night prayer. That was the tradition.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he left him-- so he-- after the war, he told him give me, share with the things which remains of the parents, what my daddy had. So he gave me the tie. After the war I met with him, and he kept the sash from the Friday night tradition, which he always used to wear. So I cherish this and I'm proud to have, and I'm glad that I was able to save through all of this traveling like we had. Like I mentioned with the other idol that I have from my mom from camp. And I think this is something-- those two items, amazing and miraculously that I saved them. They are here. Because so many other things got lost. And this, like, I would like to-- forever to remind that they-- they've been with me. They are my parents and something, remnants still will continue. And then my grandchildren, my family will cherish and save this as well as I tried to cherish this and save. OK. Mrs. Huppert, tell us what we're seeing here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Holocaust painting, which I painted when I was ill. I promised myself if I get well, I will dedicate this painting to my people and to myself. That was my prayer. I pray that God, that the Holy Name should never be take in vain and profane. And that the Almighty should watch over these people and never forsake them. The painting is representing the Holocaust, the ashes. With the-- six million perished. This is just a segment. The tallit, which represent our people, prayer shawl. And the cup, which I-- there's a reflection of a concentration camp. And you see that reflects our people, what they perished. And I feel this is the cup of bitterness. I hope this will end it forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: The holy scrolls, which they burned when they burned all the holy scriptures, all the culture, and the holy places were destroyed. During Holocaust, the Nazis destroyed everything, but they could not destroy our-- our belief. And this will live for that forever. And-- although I-- I put a lot of tears in this for 10 years, but I think at least something to go on. And there is a small segment of the writing in the scripture from the Bible to the children of Israel, Abrahams, Isaac. and Isaac. And it's the children of Abraham, Isaac, and Isaac? Jacob.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob. They should live under in the freedom and sit on their own [INAUDIBLE]. None should make them afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you Mrs. Huppert. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also the picture represent the shofar, which is, that says, [NON-ENGLISH]. It means the call of our people from God and call to God. And this is the traditional shofar, which you always use on High Holy days. And on the bottom you see a little rosebud, which represents our babies and the children didn't have a chance to-- even to bloom. They perished in such a horrible death in gas chambers.
